planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in zone 10–12; the Pemba Island Palm is a rare, clustering, feather-leaved palm endemic to Pemba Island off the coast of Tanzania — producing graceful arching fronds on slender stems from a suckering base, and one of the lesser-known gems in the Dypsis genus transplanted to tropical gardens.

directions: Plant Dypsis pembana in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in a site with bright indirect light or partial sun; protect from frost, extended drought, and waterlogged conditions; young palms prefer more shade and gradually accept more sun as they mature. The Pemba Island Palm is an attractive clustering feather palm from Pemba Island in the Zanzibar Archipelago of Tanzania — an island known for its extraordinary biodiversity and high levels of endemism. The palm forms multi-stemmed clumps of slender, graceful stems bearing arching pinnate fronds that create a lush, tropical effect in the garden; the suckering habit makes it an excellent screen plant in frost-free gardens. Like many island-endemic palms, Dypsis pembana is threatened in its native habitat due to land conversion and is rarely seen in cultivation outside of specialist palm collections; sourcing nursery-propagated material supports conservation. In zone 10–12, plant in a sheltered position with regular moisture during the establishment period; once settled, it requires only moderate supplemental irrigation.
sources: The IUCN Red List — Dypsis pembana
International Palm Society — Pemba Island Palm

planting.note: Plant tubers or seedlings after water temperatures reliably exceed 70°F in full sun in still or slow-moving water 18–36 inches deep in large ponds or water features in zone 9–12; the Santa Cruz Water Lily produces the world's largest floating leaves — circular, up to 5 feet across with upturned edges — and dramatic white to pink-blushed fragrant flowers that open at night over two successive evenings, shifting from white to pink as they age.

directions: Grow Victoria cruziana in full sun in water 18–36 inches deep; pot the rhizome in heavy, unamended clay loam (not potting mix — it floats) in a large aquatic container and submerge; apply slow-release aquatic plant fertilizer monthly and provide water temperatures above 70°F for active growth. The Santa Cruz Water Lily is the hardier of the two Victoria species — native to the rivers and backwaters of Bolivia, Paraguay, and northern Argentina — and is more forgiving than its Amazonian relative in temperate and subtropical water gardens. The enormous floating pads, edged with upturned rims 3–4 inches high, can support significant weight; the undersides are covered with sharp spines and a network of air-filled chambers. Flowers are thermogenic, producing heat to attract scarce pollinators: they open white on the first night, trapping beetle pollinators inside; on the second night they reopen as pink and release the pollen-dusted beetles. In zone 9, treat as a tender annual unless overwintered in a heated indoor pool; in zone 10–12, rhizomes may persist for multiple years. Each plant needs at least 100 square feet of open water surface.
sources: International Waterlily and Water Gardening Society — growing Victoria

subcategory: aquatic edible seed Asian water lily

planting.note: Sow seeds in shallow water or press seeds into aquatic container soil in late spring when water temperatures exceed 70°F in full sun in zone 8–12; Euryale ferox (Prickly Water Lily, Gorgon Plant, Fox Nut) is a monotypic Asian aquatic grown primarily for its starchy, popped seeds — makhana — a major crop in Bihar, India — and ornamentally for its enormous spiny floating leaves that can reach 5 feet across.

directions: Direct sow Euryale ferox seeds in warm, still, shallow water in full sun; press seeds into heavy clay loam at the pond bottom or in sunken aquatic pots, spacing plants 5–10 feet apart to accommodate the enormous leaves; fertilize with aquatic tablets monthly for maximum leaf and seed production. The Prickly Water Lily is an annual or short-lived perennial aquatic in the Victoria family, native to southeastern Asia from India to Japan — the only species in its genus. Its enormous spiny floating leaves (up to 5 feet across, dark green above and deep purple-red below), covered in sharp prickles on both surfaces, are among the most dramatic of all aquatic plants. The seeds — dried and popped to make makhana (fox nuts) — are a prized crop in the Mithila region of Bihar and widely consumed across India, Nepal, and China for their high starch content and nutty flavor. In warm climates, the plant grows extremely fast and can form a dense, nearly impenetrable floating mat; harvest seeds in late summer as seed pods ripen and begin to sink. In zone 8, it may self-sow from seeds that overwinter in pond mud; in zone 10–12, it may behave as a short-lived perennial.
sources: FAO — Euryale ferox aquaculture cultivation, India
University of Florida IFAS — aquatic edible plants

subcategory: Egyptian sacred night-blooming aquatic water lily

planting.note: Plant tubers in spring in full sun in aquatic pots with heavy clay loam under 6–18 inches of warm water in zone 9–12; the White Egyptian Lotus is a tropical night-blooming water lily native to the Nile Delta, East Africa, and Southeast Asia — one of the most ancient sacred aquatic plants in human history — bearing large, fragrant, white flowers with prominent yellow stamens that open at dusk and close by midmorning.

directions: Plant Nymphaea lotus tubers in heavy, unamended clay loam in large aquatic pots submerged under 6–18 inches of warm water in full sun; top-dress monthly with aquatic fertilizer tablets and maintain water temperatures above 65°F for active growth; lift tubers and overwinter indoors in a bucket of water in zone 9 and colder. The White Egyptian Lotus is a night-blooming tropical water lily sacred in ancient Egyptian religion and art — depicted extensively on temple walls, papyrus scrolls, and tomb paintings, where it symbolized creation, rebirth, and the sun. Unlike the Blue Lotus (Nymphaea caerulea) which opens at sunrise, Nymphaea lotus opens at dusk and closes the following morning; flowers are strongly fragrant, white to pale pink at the tips, 6–10 inches across, and held well above the water on strong stalks. The toothed, slightly wavy leaf margins distinguish Nymphaea lotus from other white water lilies. Native to the Nile Delta and East Africa, the species has also become naturalized throughout Southeast Asia where it grows wild in rice paddies and irrigation canals. In zone 9, overwinter as for other tropical water lilies; in zone 10–12, it may persist year-round in garden ponds.
sources: International Waterlily and Water Gardening Society — tropical night-bloomers

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in full sun to partial shade in still or slow-moving water 1–4 feet deep in zone 3–10; the Yellow Pond Lily (Brandy Bottle) is a vigorous, cold-hardy, native aquatic perennial found across Europe, North America, and western Asia — forming spreading colonies of large, heart-shaped, floating leaves and cheerful bright-yellow, globe-shaped flowers above the water surface from late spring through summer.

directions: Plant Nuphar lutea rhizome sections horizontally in heavy clay loam in large aquatic containers or directly in the pond bottom in 1–4 feet of still or slow-moving water; top-dress with aquatic fertilizer tablets in spring; thin colonies in fall by dividing and removing excess rhizome sections as needed. The Yellow Pond Lily is one of the most cold-tolerant and widely adaptable of all aquatic perennials — native across a broad swathe of the Northern Hemisphere from North America and Europe to western Asia — and perfectly suited to ponds and naturalistic water gardens where winters are too severe for tropical water lilies. The bright yellow, globe-shaped flowers (the old name 'Brandy Bottle' refers to their faint alcoholic scent) are held just above the water surface and attract native bees and beetles as pollinators. Large, waxy, heart-shaped leaves float on the surface, providing excellent shade that reduces algae growth and offers fish cover beneath. Unlike tropical water lilies, Yellow Pond Lily goes dormant in winter and re-emerges from thick rhizomes in spring; rhizomes can form substantial colonies over time and may need periodic thinning in smaller ponds. The seeds and rhizomes are edible and were historically used by Native Americans.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Nuphar lutea

subcategory: Gulf Coast native marginal aquatic perennial

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in full sun to partial shade in shallow water or permanently wet soil in zone 8–12; Alligator Flag (Fire-Flag) is a tall, dramatic, native marginal aquatic perennial found throughout the Gulf Coast, Caribbean, and tropical Americas — producing bold, canna-like foliage on tall stems reaching 6–8 feet and loose, zig-zagging racemes of small violet-purple flowers beloved by ruby-throated hummingbirds.

directions: Plant Thalia geniculata rhizome divisions in moist to wet soil or in the shallow margins of ponds in full sun to partial shade; space plants 3–5 feet apart and allow to form clumps; divide every 2–3 years to maintain vigor and spread to new areas. Alligator Flag is one of the most striking native marginal aquatics of the Gulf Coast and is an important wildlife plant throughout its natural range from the southeastern United States through the Caribbean and Central America into South America. The bold, upright, canna-like leaves create a dramatic vertical accent at the water's edge; the tall, arching, zigzag-jointed flower stalks bear small but intricate violet-blue flowers that are a magnet for hummingbirds throughout the warm season. In Gulf Coast gardens (zone 8–10), Alligator Flag can become a large, clump-forming colony in bog gardens, pond margins, and low wet areas; the foliage dies back to the rhizome after frost in zone 8–9 but re-emerges reliably in spring. Seeds develop in nutlet-like capsules and are relished by waterfowl. An excellent choice for naturalizing wet swales, retention pond edges, and rain gardens throughout the coastal South.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Thalia geniculata
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Thalia geniculata
